CGEC - Background

About the Center for Genomic Experimentation and Computation

The Molecular Sciences Institute Center for Genomic Experimentation and Computation (CGEC) was created to pursue interdisciplinary projects that will enable broad understanding of cellular and organismic behavior and work towards making predictive models of biological systems. This understanding will serve to radically improve researchers? abilities to understand biology by providing them with advanced methods and tools to probe important biological questions. Being a newly designated Center of Excellence in Genomic Sciences by the National Human Genome Research Institute (NHGRI) is continued acknowledgement of the MSI?s past and future research contributions to the creation of this new field of predictive biology. Joining the MSI researchers in this effort are collaborating CGEC members from the California Institute of Technology, the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, the University of California ? Berkeley, and Pacific Northwest National Laboratory.

Each Center of Excellence in Genomic Sciences is chosen by a competitive peer-review process that challenged applicants to develop highly novel and innovative research projects with the potential to bring about new experimental and computational approaches to solving biological problems for the study of human biology and disease. These Centers will also serve as world-class education and training grounds for the next generation of researchers who will continue to work and train others at this disciplinary interface. NHGRI expects to establish about 10 of these Centers, funding 2-4 grants per year. There are currently 4 other Centers of Excellence, at Yale, Stanford, and two at the University of Washington, outstanding institutions of higher education. As the first independent research laboratory to be recognized as a Center of Excellence under this program, the MSI is positioned to become one of the world leaders of the next phase of genomic research, using the advances of the Human Genome Project to develop new research methods and computational tools that will enable a deeper understanding of biology.

About the Alpha Project

The flagship project at the MSI Center for Genomic Experimentation and Computation is the Alpha Project. The focus of the Alpha Project is to examine extra- to intra-cellular information flow and processing. These processes are how cells receive, amplify, and integrate signals from a variety of stimuli. Signal transduction is an integral part of most cellular processes. Quantifying how cells sense and respond to various stimuli and creating a model that will accurately predict these responses are the first important steps to understanding diseases and eventually to tailoring precise treatments to each individual. Gaining this level of understanding will require the development of new research methods and computational tools to probe and analyze such complex biological processes.

The MSI chose a simple prototype cellular information-processing pathway for this five-year study, the pheromone signal transduction pathway in yeast. This pathway mediates yeast cells response to the mating pheromone signal from cells of the opposite mating type, thereby controlling the genetic and physiological response of yeast cells to a particular stimulus in their environment. The bakers yeast, Saccharomyces cerevisiae, was chosen for study because it is a simple unicellular organism and is one of the most well studied in biology. It is easy to manipulate in the lab, its genome has been sequenced, the genes have been identified, and it has decades of physiological data attached to it. But, as much as scientists have learned about yeast, they still do not know enough to be able to make accurate predictions about its behavior, nor are they able to aim chemical treatments precisely and understand the effects those treatments will have. Studying how yeast cells process information helps us understand information processing in higher organisms because the molecular components involved in these processes share many similarities. The Alpha Project is reductively focused on genes in a relatively small biological pathway (about 25 genes) in a particular model organism and ultimately could have vast implications for studying how genes work in more complex organisms, including humans.

The project has three main goals: 1) to develop experimental approaches to measure individual components of the signal transduction pathway, at the population and single yeast cell levels, 2) to develop computational tools to make computer simulations of the behavior of the pathway and the cell, and 3) to combine both experimental approaches and computational tools to build models that predict the behavior of cells in response to defined changes.

These goals will be addressed by two large research teams, led by Dr. Roger Brent, President and Director of the MSI: the Genomic Methods Development team and the Simulation Methods Development team. (A third team, the Administration team, will manage the operations of the project.) The GMD team is tasked with developing the necessary experimental tools and methods needed to make sensitive, quantitative measurements (such as the level of protein expression, the type and level of protein modifications, etc.) The results from the GMD will include novel research detection methods that will be useful to researchers around the world and significant amounts of data that will contribute to a greater understanding of not only this particular pathway, but of aspects of the biology of cells in general. GMD is being led by a group of researchers who are world experts in advanced genomic techniques and in developing new research methods: Dr. Orna Resnekov (MSI), Dr. Alejandro Colman-Lerner (MSI), and Dr. Richard Smith (Pacific Northwest National Laboratory), including a collaboration with Dr. Julie Leary (UC Berkeley).

The SMD team will work closely with and integrate data from the GMD team to develop mathematical/computational representations of the pathway, simulating its behavior in silico. This team is led by Dr. Larry Lok (MSI), Dr. Drew Endy (MIT) and Dr. Shuki Bruck (CalTech), all leaders in computational biology. Lab and computer simulation data will be integrated into a core model of the pathway which, when successfully completed, will represent a leap in our understanding of how cells behave in response to external and internal signals.

Another unique feature of this project is that team members will have the opportunity to learn and work in the varied research environments represented in the CGEC ? an independent research lab, university departments, and a national lab ? creating an outstanding training ground for multidisciplinary researchers. It is this close coordination and integrated effort that will be one of the hallmarks of this project and of the MSI. Learning how to successfully operate such an elaborate, multidisciplinary research effort will be an additional benefit from the Alpha Project, resulting in lessons about the practice of interdisciplinary research that can be used by similar efforts in other institutions. The CGEC is also committed to the practice of rapid data release, to provide the technologies, methods, data and software its researchers generate to the scientific community as quickly as possible.

Future projects similar in innovation, scope and magnitude are already in the planning stages at the CGEC. A logical next step from the Alpha Project is the extension of this research to higher organisms. Also being considered are projects dealing with spatial representations of biological molecules and making simulations of evolutionary processes. This grant from the NHGRI to fund the Alpha Project allows the CGEC to look forward to increasing its group of outstanding multidisciplinary researchers and to continued advancement of genomic technology to bear on other important biological problems.
